title: Migraines as Suppressed Anger Conversations
dek: *When you swallow your fury for the hundredth time, your nervous system doesn't forget. It files the charge somewhere, and for many of us, that somewhere is the delicate vasculature wrapped around the brain. This is about what happens when rage becomes a headache, and how speaking changes the physiology.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Migraines often emerge when anger has nowhere to go except inward, turning unsaid words into physical pain. The body stores what the mouth won't speak, and the head becomes the pressure valve. Learning to have the difficult conversation before the headache arrives changes everything.

How many times have you bitten your tongue this week, felt the heat rise in your chest, then watched it sink back down into silence? In my own practice, I've watched the pattern repeat itself with startling consistency. Someone comes in describing debilitating migraines. We talk about triggers. They list wine, cheese, weather changes. Then, almost as an afterthought, they mention the meeting where they said nothing. The dinner where they smiled through gritted teeth. The text message they rewrote six times before deleting it entirely. The migraine arrived eight hours later, right on schedule. Your body is not a filing cabinet where difficult emotions disappear into neat folders. It's a living, responsive system that processes everything you don't say out loud. And when anger has no external outlet, it turns inward with remarkable precision, often landing in the head as a full-blown migraine. ## The Physiology of Swallowed Rage Let's get specific about what happens inside you when you suppress anger. Your autonomic nervous system doesn't distinguish between a threat you're running from and a confrontation you're avoiding. Both read as danger. Both trigger the same cascade. Your blood pressure rises. Cortisol floods your system. The blood vessels in your brain constrict, then dilate in a rebound effect. This vascular instability is the mechanical heart of many migraines. But the trigger isn't always tyramine or barometric pressure. Sometimes it's the conversation you refused to have at lunch. Psychologist Harriet Lerner spent decades studying how women, in particular, are taught to suppress anger in the name of maintaining relationships. She observed that this suppression doesn't make the anger vanish. It redirects it. The body becomes the messenger for what the mouth won't deliver. Over the years I've seen this pattern play out in people of all genders, but the cultural conditioning around "niceness" creates a particular vulnerability. The inflammation markers that spike during a migraine attack are the same ones that elevate during acute stress. Your immune system is responding to a perceived threat. And when that threat is an unspoken boundary violation or an unexpressed resentment, your body still mobilizes as if you're under attack. Because, in a very real sense, you are. ## When the Head Becomes the Battleground There's a reason the pain lands where it does. The trigeminal nerve, which plays a central role in migraine pain, is also intimately connected to the limbic system, the emotional processing center of your brain. Anger that can't move through you verbally often moves through you neurologically instead. Beverly Engel, who has written extensively on the relationship between unexpressed emotion and physical symptoms, points out that the body will always find a way to communicate what we refuse to acknowledge consciously. The migraine becomes the voice you wouldn't let yourself use. The throbbing becomes the emphasis you didn't allow in your actual words. In our experience working with people who suffer chronic migraines, there's almost always a parallel conversation that never happened. The job where they stay silent about unfair workloads. The relationship where they absorb criticism without pushback. The family dynamic where their needs come last, every single time. The headache is the period at the end of the sentence you didn't say. Your skull feels like it's splitting because, metaphorically, you are splitting. Part of you knows the truth. Part of you is enforcing silence. The migraine is what happens in that unbearable gap between knowing and speaking. ## The Anger That Hides in Plain Sight Not all anger announces itself with raised voices or slammed doors. Some of it arrives as a tight jaw. A clenched stomach. A headache that starts at the base of your skull and climbs. Robert Augustus Masters distinguishes between what he calls "unilluminated anger" and anger that's been brought into conscious awareness. Unilluminated anger operates in the shadows. It leaks out as passive aggression, chronic tension, and yes, somatic symptoms like migraines. When you illuminate it, when you name it and give it words, the charge dissipates. This doesn't mean every migraine is suppressed anger. But if you track your headaches against your emotional landscape, you might notice a correlation. The migraine that arrives the day after you smiled through a boundary violation. The one that shows up every Sunday night before you return to a job you resent. The pattern that emerges after family gatherings where you perform a version of yourself that isn't quite true. Your body is trying to tell you something. The question is whether you're ready to listen. Many people reach for [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) when the tension starts to build, and there's wisdom in supporting your nervous system during acute stress. But the deeper work is learning to speak before the pressure becomes unbearable. ## The Conversation You Keep Not Having Let's do something practical right now. Think of the last migraine you had. Now rewind twelve to forty-eight hours before it started. What was happening in your life? Who were you with? What didn't you say? Write it down. Not the polite version. The real one. The words that felt too sharp, too honest, too risky. Write the conversation you would have had if you weren't afraid of the consequences. You don't have to send it. You don't even have to read it again. But notice what happens in your body as you write. Does your jaw unclench? Does your breathing deepen? Does the pressure behind your eyes shift even slightly? This is information. Your system has been waiting for permission to tell the truth, even if it's just on paper, even if no one else ever sees it. Across the articles we've published on this site, one theme emerges consistently: the body keeps score, and it will eventually demand payment. You can pay in small installments, difficult conversations had in real time, or you can pay in migraines, muscle tension, and chronic pain. The bill comes due either way. ## Building a Practice of Timely Truth The goal isn't to become someone who explodes at every provocation. It's to become someone who can speak difficult truths before they calcify into physical symptoms. This requires practice. Start small. Notice when you feel heat in your chest or tightness in your throat. These are early warning signs. Your body is saying, "This matters. This needs words." Instead of swallowing it, try saying something simple: "I need a minute to think about that." Or, "That doesn't work for me." Or even just, "Hm." These small acts of honesty create space. They interrupt the pattern of immediate suppression. They buy you time to figure out what you actually want to say. Some people find that working with [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) helps them reconnect with their capacity for healthy anger. Fire energy, when it's flowing properly, doesn't accumulate as migraine pain. It moves through you as clear communication, appropriate boundaries, and the willingness to say no. Others benefit from nervous system support during the learning curve. A drop of [Planetary Herbals Bupleurum Liver Cleanse (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0002YFGBM?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help your liver process the stress hormones that spike when you're learning to speak up. In traditional Chinese medicine, the liver is intimately connected to anger, and supporting it physically can make the emotional work more manageable. The practice is this: when you notice the early signs of suppressed anger, before the migraine sets in, take five minutes. Sit down. Put your hand on your chest. Ask yourself, "What am I not saying?" Then write it. Speak it out loud to an empty room. Tell it to a trusted friend. The medium matters less than the movement from internal to external. ## The Body's Remarkable Memory Your nervous system has been tracking patterns since you were very young. It knows which emotions were safe to express and which ones brought punishment, withdrawal, or chaos. If anger wasn't welcome in your family of origin, your body learned to suppress it automatically, often before it even reached conscious awareness. This is why the migraine can feel like it comes from nowhere. You're not aware of swallowing your anger because you've been doing it for so long that it's become autonomic. The body remembers what the mind has forgotten. Healing this pattern requires working with both levels. You need the conscious practice of speaking difficult truths. And you need the somatic work of helping your nervous system understand that it's safe to feel anger now, even if it wasn't safe when you were seven. Some people find that gentle, repetitive touch helps. Placing your hand on your jaw, your throat, your chest while you breathe and acknowledge the anger can signal to your system that you're not in danger. Others work with practitioners who understand trauma-informed bodywork. The goal is to create new neural pathways where anger can move through you without triggering a full migraine response. You might also explore [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) for allies that support this rewiring. [Planetary Herbals Bupleurum Liver Cleanse (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0002YFGBM?tag=spankyspinola-20), for instance, has been used traditionally to help move stuck energy, particularly the kind that shows up as tension headaches and irritability. The body's memory is long, but it's also remarkably plastic. With consistent practice, you can teach your system a new response. Anger doesn't have to become a migraine. It can become words, boundaries, and the kind of clarity that actually prevents pain instead of causing it. ## What Changes When You Speak Here's what I've observed happens when people start having the conversations they've been avoiding. The migraines don't always disappear immediately. But their frequency changes. Their intensity shifts. And crucially, the person starts to feel a sense of agency that wasn't there before. Instead of being ambushed by pain, they start to see the connection. They notice the pattern. They can feel the anger rising and make a choice about how to express it before it becomes a three-day migraine that requires a dark room and ice packs. This doesn't mean every conversation goes well. Some of them are messy. Some relationships can't hold the truth. But even when the external outcome isn't ideal, the internal outcome shifts. You've spoken. The charge has moved. Your body doesn't have to carry it anymore. According to the APA's research summary on anger management, unexpressed anger is associated with a range of physical health problems, including hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and chronic pain conditions. The research consistently shows that people who can express anger in healthy, direct ways have better health outcomes than those who suppress it or express it destructively. The middle path is honest, timely communication. Not rage. Not silence. Something in between. For more context on how we approach emotional work at the intersection of body and spirit, you might explore [the about page](/about), where we talk about the lineage and philosophy that informs this work. ## A Five-Minute Practice: The Anger Inventory Set a timer for five minutes. Get out a piece of paper or open a blank document. At the top, write: "Things I'm angry about that I haven't said out loud." Then write. Don't edit. Don't make it pretty. Don't worry about whether it's "justified" or "too small to matter." If it's in your body, it matters. Write until the timer goes off. When you're done, read through the list. Notice which items carry the most charge. Those are your candidates for actual conversations. You don't have to have all of them today. But knowing what they are changes your relationship to the migraines when they come. Some people like to burn the list afterward. Others keep it as a reference. Do what feels right. The point is to externalize what's been circulating internally. This practice, done regularly, can interrupt the migraine cycle before it starts. It gives your anger somewhere to go that isn't your skull. If you're working with this alongside other practices, you might find that something like [Planetary Herbals Bupleurum Liver Cleanse (alt SKU)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0002YFGBM?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps support the process, particularly if you're also dealing with digestive issues or irritability. The liver in Chinese medicine is where anger gets stuck, and supporting it can ease the transition as you learn to express more directly. You might also explore the resources at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) for additional practices that work with emotional energy in the body. ## Three Questions People Actually Ask ### Can a migraine really be caused by not speaking up? Not every migraine has an emotional component, but many do. The vascular changes that characterize migraines can be triggered by the stress response that activates when you suppress strong emotions. If you notice a pattern where migraines follow situations where you didn't say what you needed to say, that's worth paying attention to. The body doesn't separate emotional stress from physical stress. Both create the same inflammatory cascade that can lead to migraine pain. ### What if speaking up makes things worse? This is a valid fear, and sometimes speaking up does change relationships in ways that are uncomfortable. But consider the cost of not speaking. Chronic migraines, resentment that builds over years, a life shaped by what you couldn't say. There's risk in both directions. The question is which risk you're willing to take. Start with lower-stakes conversations. Practice with people who are safe. Build your capacity gradually. You don't have to have the hardest conversation first. ### How do I know if my anger is justified or if I'm just being difficult? This question itself often signals that you've internalized the message that your anger isn't valid. Anger is information. It tells you when a boundary has been crossed, when something matters to you, when a situation isn't working. Whether it's "justified" is less important than whether it's real. If you feel it, it's worth examining. You can feel anger and choose not to act on it, but dismissing it as "being difficult" usually just drives it underground where it becomes a migraine instead of a conversation. ## The Headache That Becomes a Voice There's a moment in the progression of a migraine where you can feel it gathering. The aura. The sensitivity to light. The sense that something is building behind your eyes. Most people who get migraines know this feeling intimately. What if, in that moment, instead of reaching immediately for medication or a dark room, you asked yourself: What am I not saying? Who am I angry at? What conversation have I been avoiding? Sometimes the answer is immediate and obvious. Sometimes it takes sitting quietly for a few minutes to surface. But when it comes, when you can name it, something shifts. Not always enough to stop the migraine entirely, but enough to change your relationship to it. The pain becomes information instead of punishment. Your body is trying to protect you, trying to signal that something needs attention. When you listen, when you take the message seriously, the messenger doesn't have to shout as loud next time. This is slow work. It requires patience with yourself and a willingness to fumble through conversations you've never practiced having. But over time, the pattern changes. The migraines become less frequent. The ones that do come feel different, less like an ambush and more like a signal you can read. You learn to speak before the pressure builds. You learn that your voice, even when it shakes, is safer than silence. And your body, relieved of its burden as the only truth-teller, begins to relax in ways you didn't know were possible. *Tool for this work: [Aura Cacia Organic Lavender (0.25 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B003N43A4I?tag=spankyspinola-20).* ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on migraines as suppressed anger conversations, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
